topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded lines of the old woodworking plans on the dusty workbench. The scent of aged cedar and linseed oil, once a comfort, now felt like a suffocating shroud. His son, Leo, was a whirlwind of tiny, insistent hands and an almost frightening focus. Every afternoon, the workshop, once his sanctuary, was now a bustling construction site dedicated entirely to miniature replicas of the birds he’d carved years ago. Leo didn't ask; he simply took over, meticulously sanding down the wings of a wren with a level of precision that bordered on obsessive. Elias watched from the doorway, his shoulders slumping with a profound weariness. He felt a dull ache settle behind his ribs, a persistent hollowness that no amount of careful sanding could fill. "Leo," Elias managed, his voice raspy, "perhaps we could work on something else today?" Leo looked up, his eyes bright and unwavering, a tiny smudge of sawdust decorating his cheek. "But Papa," he whispered, his tone utterly serious, "the robin needs a perfect beak."

[story 2]

The silence in the studio was deafening, broken only by the faint, rhythmic scraping of tiny chisels against wood. Clara stared at the unfinished wooden sculpture—a complex, miniature depiction of a sea creature. It was beautiful, undeniably so, but it wasn't hers. It was Leo’s latest masterpiece, a painstakingly detailed rendition of a deep-sea anglerfish. Clara found herself leaning against the doorframe, her breath catching in shallow gasps. She remembered the effortless joy she used to find in shaping wood, the meditative calm that used to wash over her. Now, every time she looked at her tools, she saw only the ghost of her own past satisfaction, overshadowed by the relentless, consuming energy radiating from her son. A tightness gripped her chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. "It’s magnificent, sweetie," she said, forcing a bright tone that felt brittle on her tongue. Leo didn't look up, his concentration absolute. "It has to be perfect, Mama. The light catches the lure just so."

[story 3]

Mark kept glancing at the collection of antique clock mechanisms on the shelf. They were his escape, the intricate dance of gears and springs offering a predictable, soothing order to his chaotic life. But lately, that order had been hijacked. His twelve-year-old, Finn, had developed an intense fascination with horology, transforming the quiet corner of the garage into a high-stakes workshop. Finn didn't just look at the clocks; he disassembled them, cleaned the minuscule pivots with surgical care, and spent hours trying to understand the escapement. Mark felt a strange detachment, as if he were observing a foreign, intensely focused ritual. When Finn finally presented him with a newly assembled pocket watch, its movement a hypnotic shimmer, Mark felt a profound sense of isolation. He managed a weak smile, but his gaze drifted away, fixed on the dusty floor. "That's... very intricate, Finn," he murmured, the words sounding hollow even to his own ears. Finn beamed, a triumphant expression that didn't quite reach his eyes, completely absorbed in the perfect tick.
